name: NestJS
what_it_does: Opinionated Node.js framework using TypeScript decorators and Angular-inspired module/provider/controller architecture with built-in dependency injection, OpenAPI generation, and first-class support for REST, GraphQL, and microservices.
use_cases: Build strongly-typed REST APIs with auto-generated OpenAPI/Swagger docs using @Controller, @Get/@Post decorators and class-validator DTOs
Use NestJS dependency injection to wire services, repositories, and providers with testable, loosely-coupled architecture patterns
Generate a GraphQL API using NestJS's code-first approach with @ObjectType and @Resolver decorators that auto-build the schema from TypeScript classes
Implement microservices communication using NestJS transport layers (Redis, NATS, RabbitMQ, Kafka) with a consistent decorator-based API
Use Guards, Interceptors, and Pipes as decorator-based middleware to implement authentication, response transformation, and input validation in a reusable, composable way
not_for: Simple CRUD APIs or prototypes where NestJS's boilerplate (modules, providers, decorators) adds setup friction compared to Express or Fastify
Teams unfamiliar with TypeScript and decorator patterns — NestJS's DI system and module structure have a steep learning curve for JavaScript newcomers
Serverless functions requiring minimal cold-start time — NestJS module initialization adds overhead that Lambda/Edge functions are too short-lived to amortize
best_when: You're building a large-scale TypeScript Node.js API where structured architecture, built-in DI, auto-generated OpenAPI docs, and Angular-style patterns matter.
avoid_when: You need a minimal footprint Node.js API, are using plain JavaScript, or are deploying to serverless environments with strict cold-start budgets.

auth.notes: NestJS is an npm package requiring no external auth. Applications implement auth via Passport.js integration, JWT Guards, or custom AuthGuards using the @UseGuards() decorator.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Unlimited, open source under MIT license
pricing.paid_tiers: NestJS Enterprise offering provides official support and consulting; framework is free
pricing.notes: MIT-licensed and free; NestJS has a commercial enterprise support tier but the framework itself has no feature gating.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: NestJS provides structured error messages with HTTP status codes and clear DI resolution failures; Nest's 'Nest can't resolve dependencies of X' errors include the injection token and module context.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No built-in idempotency; agents must implement it at the service/repository layer using upsert patterns with TypeORM or Prisma.
known_agent_gotchas: Circular dependency between modules causes a cryptic 'Cannot read properties of undefined' error — agents must use forwardRef() or restructure module imports to break the cycle

NestJS module scope (DEFAULT singleton vs REQUEST vs TRANSIENT) is easy to get wrong; agents injecting request-scoped providers into singleton services creates a scope mismatch that silently reuses stale state
Decorator metadata requires 'emitDecoratorMetadata: true' and 'experimentalDecorators: true' in tsconfig.json — agents scaffolding projects without these settings will see all DI fail silently

Exception filters, guards, and interceptors must be bound at the correct scope (method, controller, global) — agents applying them globally via useGlobalGuards() bypass module-level dependency injection for providers

The @Body() decorator without a ValidationPipe does not validate or transform DTOs by default — agents generating controllers must explicitly configure the pipe globally or per-endpoint to prevent raw unvalidated objects reaching service methods

security_score_components.security_notes: NestJS has no built-in secret management; ConfigModule with .env files is the standard pattern. Helmet.js integration is recommended for HTTP security headers. No automatic CSRF protection — must be added manually for session-based apps.
